title: Handoff ritual — two ownership modes + public checklist
problem: The portal's FAQ promises that the client owns the code, accounts, and domain at the end of any engagement ("À toi. Le repo Git, les comptes, le domaine — tout."). That promise is the load-bearing positioning move of the practice — it inverts the typical agency lock-in model and self-selects clients who value autonomy. But the promise was a single FAQ line: no operational backing, no public procedure, no answer to "what if I want a managed mode where I don't deal with the keys?" and no kill-switch if Marc becomes unreachable. A skeptical Quebec buyer (notary, accountant, tradesperson) cannot verify the promise before signing. This feature operationalizes the promise: two named ownership modes ("Tout à toi" / "Je m'en occupe"), a public /handoff page that explains them, a public /handoff/checklist page with the exact transfer procedure (commands, accounts, steps), and a 90-day dormancy kill-switch documented inside that checklist. The checklist being public is the trust signal — a buyer can read the exact transfer procedure without asking.
analysis: The FAQ entry at src/i18n.ts:255-256 (FR) and ~869 (EN) made an unconditional promise that the client owns everything at the end. Discussion with Marc confirmed the practice wants to offer two modes, not just one: a default 'Tout à toi' where the client holds the keys from day 1, and an optional 'Je m'en occupe' where Marc is custodian for an annual fee. The second mode must preserve the no-lock-in promise — non-payment auto-reverts to 'Tout à toi' rather than cutting off service, and a 90-day dormancy procedure lets the client take ownership unilaterally if Marc becomes unreachable. The checklist that describes every transferable asset (repo, domain, hosting, D1, Resend, secrets) is published at /handoff/checklist so the buyer can verify the promise themselves before signing. This is implemented as two public pages following the Privacy.tsx pattern (local COPY const, Header+Footer wrap), a soft preference radio on the intake form (writes to formData under the reserved __handoff_mode key), and a Tier 2 pricing addendum that surfaces the two modes at the moment a buyer is evaluating tiers.
implementation: Two public pages built: src/pages/Handoff.tsx (mode comparison, transferable assets, ritual steps, FAQ-style addenda, switching policy, dormancy section) and src/pages/HandoffChecklist.tsx (9-section operational procedure with exact commands per asset). Both follow the Privacy.tsx pattern with local bilingual COPY consts. Routes wired in src/router.tsx: /handoff, /en/handoff, /handoff/checklist, /en/handoff/checklist — all lazy-loaded. The Tier 2 card in src/components/Pricing.tsx renders a one-line addendum below the anchor card pointing to /handoff (copy lives in src/i18n.ts under pricing.tier2Note + pricing.tier2NoteCta, bilingual). src/components/Footer.tsx gains a 'Comment ça finit / How it ends' link alongside the Privacy link. src/components/About.tsx adds a one-paragraph ownership note above the outbound-links list. src/components/intake/TypeForm.tsx adds an optional 3-option radio (Tout à toi / Je m'en occupe / On en parle plus tard, default 'on-en-parle') that writes to formData under the reserved key __handoff_mode. src/components/intake/IntakeSummary.tsx renders the chosen mode in the summary if present. Existing FAQ entries at src/i18n.ts:255-256 (FR) and the EN twin were rewritten to point visitors at the new /handoff page. Operational mirror written at docs/handoff/CHECKLIST.md (internal, kept in sync with the public page on each change). CSS added at the end of src/styles.css under a .handoff-* namespace: two-column mode grid, asset definition list, numbered step list, FAQ accordion using native <details>, plus the Tier 2 addendum and About ownership-note styles.

decisions.decision: Offer two modes (Tout à toi default, Je m'en occupe at $200/yr) instead of a single unconditional 'you own it'
decisions.rationale: Some non-technical Quebec buyers explicitly do NOT want to deal with domain renewals, TLS certificates, security patches — they want the deliverable to keep working without thinking about it. Forcing them into 'Tout à toi' creates a different lock-in (the deliverable rots from neglect). Offering Managed mode lets Marc honestly charge for the boring custodial labor without breaking the no-lock-in promise, because non-payment auto-reverts to Tout à toi rather than cutting service.
alternativesConsidered: Single unconditional 'Tout à toi' mode (rejected: ignores buyers who want a managed offering)
Tiered SaaS-like subscription with multiple Managed plans (rejected: contradicts the boring-tech mandate and 1-active-cap philosophy)
Free Managed with no annual fee (rejected: creates a silent liability of dormant managed clients Marc still owes domain renewals to)

decisions.decision: Publish the operational checklist publicly at /handoff/checklist, not as an internal-only doc
decisions.rationale: A skeptical buyer (notary, accountant, small-business owner) is the target. They can verify the no-lock-in promise themselves by reading the exact transfer procedure before signing. Hiding the checklist behind 'ask Marc for details' weakens the trust signal; publishing it makes the promise inspectable. The same content lives at docs/handoff/CHECKLIST.md as an offline mirror for Marc and for clients who want a printable / non-JS version.
alternativesConsidered: Internal-only Markdown doc (rejected: buyer cannot verify without asking)
Public page behind a 'request access' gate (rejected: adds friction and looks like marketing theater)

decisions.decision: Document a 90-day dormancy kill-switch inside the public checklist (section 8)
decisions.rationale: Managed mode is only honest if the client has a documented path to ownership when Marc is unreachable. Without a kill-switch, 'Je m'en occupe' is structurally a lock-in regardless of intent — if Marc disappears, the client can't get to the repo or domain. Section 8 of the checklist names the dormancy threshold (90 days, 3 unanswered emails 30+ days apart), the support tickets to open (GitHub, Cloudflare, registrar), and the order of operations. It exists in printed form in the client's documents at delivery — the client doesn't have to ask anyone to execute it.
alternativesConsidered: Trust-based, no documented procedure (rejected: client cannot recover ownership)
Escrow service holding a copy of credentials (rejected: introduces a third party, contradicts simplicity)
Auto-handoff after non-payment only, no separate dormancy path (rejected: payment is independent of reachability — a client could pay AND still be unable to reach Marc)

decisions.decision: Mode preference is a soft radio on intake, confirmed hard at handoff
decisions.rationale: Forcing the choice at intake friction-loads the form for visitors (a roofer at 9pm Sunday doesn't know what 'managed mode' means yet). Confirming only at handoff means the choice is made after the buyer has seen the deliverable and understood the trade-offs. The intake radio defaults to 'On en parle plus tard' so it's a non-blocking optional field; the value goes into formData under the reserved key __handoff_mode and is surfaced in the IntakeSummary for both client and admin.
alternativesConsidered: Required choice at intake (rejected: friction without information)
No intake field; ask only at delivery (rejected: misses cases where the buyer has a clear preference upfront, e.g. 'I want full ownership, that's why I picked Marc')

successCriteria: (1) /handoff and /en/handoff render the two-mode comparison and reach the FAQ + dormancy sections; (2) /handoff/checklist and /en/handoff/checklist render all 9 procedural sections including section 8 dormancy kill-switch; (3) Footer shows the new 'Comment ça finit / How it ends' link on every page; (4) Pricing Tier 2 card surfaces the tier2Note addendum with a working link to /handoff; (5) Intake TypeForm shows the optional handoff mode radio at the bottom of every problem type; (6) IntakeSummary renders the chosen mode if set; (7) About page shows the ownership-note paragraph linking to /handoff; (8) docs/handoff/CHECKLIST.md mirrors the public page content; (9) npm run check passes (typecheck + lint + tests + build).
userGuide: For a buyer evaluating the practice: read /handoff first to understand the two modes (Tout à toi default, Je m'en occupe at $200/yr managed). Read /handoff/checklist if you want to verify the exact transfer procedure before signing — every transferable asset is listed with the exact commands. The dormancy section (8) documents how to take ownership unilaterally if Marc becomes unreachable for 90 days. On the intake form, the 'Préférence de gestion' radio is optional and defaults to 'On en parle plus tard' — you can decide later at delivery. For Marc operationally: when an engagement closes, walk docs/handoff/CHECKLIST.md asset by asset with the client during a 30-min session, tag v1.0-handoff on the final commit, send the recap email template at the bottom of the doc, and update the engagement's feature.json to status=shipped with a handed-off annotation noting client name + date.
knownLimitations: Public checklist is currently hand-mirrored to docs/handoff/CHECKLIST.md; drift between the two is possible until a build step generates the markdown from the COPY const.
The $200/yr Managed price is set without a single signed Managed client to calibrate against — may need adjustment after the first real engagement.

The intake __handoff_mode radio writes into formData but admin views (AdminInbox session details) render formData generically; the friendly label mapping in IntakeSummary only applies on visitor-side surfaces — admin sees the raw 'tout-a-toi' / 'je-men-occupe' / 'on-en-parle' value.

No automated test verifies that a client following the dormancy procedure can actually recover ownership end-to-end — the procedure relies on GitHub/Cloudflare/registrar support workflows that cannot be safely exercised in CI.
Pricing card addendum only renders on the anchor (Tier 2) card; if Marc later decides Managed mode applies to Tier 3 as well, the rendering logic needs to widen.

revisions.author: marc+claude
revisions.reason: Initial fill — captures the two-mode ownership decision (Tout à toi default + Je m'en occupe at $200/yr), the public-checklist trust strategy, the 90-day dormancy kill-switch, and the soft-radio intake preference. Built across Handoff.tsx, HandoffChecklist.tsx, router.tsx (4 new routes), Footer/Pricing/About link surfaces, TypeForm radio + IntakeSummary display, and docs/handoff/CHECKLIST.md mirror.

revisions.author: marc+claude
revisions.reason: Simplify the checklist for the actual audience. Page restructured into a 5-step plain-language summary + a single collapsed <details> block for asset-by-asset technical commands ('pour ton prochain dev'). Procedure mechanics simplified: (1) GPG-encrypted D1 dumps moved from mandatory to opt-in — 1Password shared file is the default share; (2) two git tags (v1.0-handoff + v1.0-handoff-accepted-YYYY-MM-DD) collapsed to one — the recap email is the acceptance record; (3) dormancy threshold dropped from 90 days × 3 emails to 30 days × 1 unanswered email — less legalistic, still operationally honest. FAQ ownership entry now carries a clickable link (FAQ.tsx switched to dangerouslySetInnerHTML, mirroring Privacy.tsx pattern). Sitemap script updated to include /handoff and /handoff/checklist (FR + EN), bringing total from 14 to 18 URLs.
